text: We present the case of a diagnostic laparoscopy with a desiolysis for intestinal obstruction in a patient with status post open hysterectomy.
Forty-two year old patient, a post open hysterectomy done one week back, presented with abdominal distension, nausea, vomiting, constipation and obstipation since post op day two.
Patient was tender, guarded and distended.
X-ray was suggestive of air fluid levels with no gas under diaphragm.
The decision was taken to do an urgent diagnostic laparoscopy in view of adhesive intestinal obstruction.
The surgeon enters the abdomen using a varizine insufflation technique at Palmer's point, followed by the placement of two working ports on the left flank.
The surgeon identifies and carefully dissects multiple omental adhesions attached to the anterior abdominal wall, separating them to relieve obstruction.
Following the completion of omental adhesiolysis, the surgeon observes a collapsed loop of the small bowel located near the vaginal vault.
The surgeon performs a short dissection to take down flimsy omental adhesions attached to the small bowel loops, revealing the underlying intestinal structures.
The surgeon identifies the left ovary and proceeds to dissect and remove omental adhesions attached to it.
The surgeon moves the omentum to one side, revealing and examining the distended small bowel beneath.
The surgeon traces the distended small bowel, initially moving proximally and then distally, to visually identify the site of the transition zone.
The surgeon uses graspers to carefully hold the distended bowel loops, ensuring no perforation occurs during manipulation.
The surgeon traces the distended small bowel distally and identifies the transition zone at the level of the vaginal vault, where possible adhesions are observed, potentially causing obstruction.
The surgeon carefully releases adhesions from the small bowel, using precise techniques to separate the bowel loops from surrounding tissue without causing damage.
A hemostatic agent is visible over the vaginal vault, potentially contributing to the formation of adhesions in this area.
A dense fibrotic band of adhesions is visible on the left lateral side of the vaginal vault, likely resulting from previous suture repair.
The surgeon attempts to dissect a thick fibrotic band of adhesions on the left lateral side of the vaginal vault using cold scissors, encountering difficulty due to the density of the tissue.

The surgeon attempts a different approach to take down the dense fibrotic adhesions on the left lateral side of the vaginal vault and release the small bowel loop, using careful dissection techniques.
The video shows sutures placed over the vaginal vault, with a hemostatic agent applied to the area, likely used to control bleeding during the initial surgery.
The surgeon performs further adhesiolysis to release the small bowel from around the vaginal vault and the right ovary, tracing it distally up to the ileocecal junction.
The surgeon performs intervowel adhesiolysis using a combination of sharp and blunt dissection techniques to release the small bowel, allowing it to be traced up to the distal ileocecal junction.
The surgeon traces the collapsed small bowel loops distally to the ileocecal junction, which is visible in the surgical field.
The surgeon examines a section of the small bowel, initially suspecting a stricture, but upon closer inspection, it is revealed to be a coiled loop of the bowel.
The surgeon inspects the vaginal vault, checking for any loose sutures, with visible hemostatic agent applied to control bleeding.
The surgeon identifies a small indirect left inguinal hernia and proceeds with suture repair, visually inspecting the hernia site and preparing for closure.
A suture repair was attempted and not a mesh repair in view of the acute intestinal obstruction and elevated counts which were pointing towards a septic state.
The surgeon carefully takes thin bites of the peritoneum during suturing to avoid injuring the underlying nerves, ensuring precise handling of the tissue.
After completing the suture repair, the surgeon places a drain in the abdominal cavity and proceeds to desufflate the abdomen, reducing the intra-abdominal pressure.
The patient was started on a clear liquid diet on post-op day one, passed flatus and stools on post-op day two, and was switched to a soft diet on post-op day three and discharged.
Thank you for watching.
